text.title: Recognition Aid Implementation Patterns
text.summary: Concrete implementation patterns for reducing recall burden: visible options, contextual information, autocomplete, and recent items.

rule.statement: Expose primary actions in a visible toolbar or navigation; reserve menus for secondary and infrequent actions.
rule.rationale: Hidden menus require users to remember the menu exists and what it contains — double recall burden. Visible toolbars convert both to recognition.

example.note: Visible tabs for main sections of an app rather than a hamburger menu that hides all navigation behind a tap.
All actions behind right-click context menu — users must remember right-click exists and recall what actions are available.

rule.statement: In multi-step flows, show a persistent summary of all prior selections visible alongside the current step.
rule.rationale: Without a context panel, users must remember what they entered on previous screens. A sidebar showing prior selections eliminates the most common cross-screen recall failure.

example.note: Checkout step 3 shows: Selected items, shipping address entered on step 1, and payment method selected on step 2 — all visible in a sidebar summary.

rule.statement: Pair every icon with a text label; use tooltips only in space-constrained areas where label cannot be shown inline.
rule.rationale: Icon-only interfaces require users to memorize icon meanings. Text labels eliminate this entirely. Tooltips are acceptable fallback when screen space prevents inline labels.

rule.statement: Keep tooltips at 7 words or fewer; show them on hover with a 300ms delay to avoid noise.
rule.rationale: Long tooltips that appear instantly become visual noise that users learn to ignore. Short, delayed tooltips are read when needed and dismissed otherwise.

rule.statement: Add autocomplete to every search and command input; show at least 5 recent items immediately on focus.
rule.rationale: Autocomplete is the single highest-ROI recognition aid. Search with suggestions converts blank-field recall to candidate recognition. Recent items list surfaces past actions without requiring memory retrieval.

rule.statement: Recent items must update immediately after use; a stale list is worse than no list.
rule.rationale: A stale list teaches users the feature is unreliable, causing them to stop using it. Immediate update maintains trust and utility.

rule.statement: Show essential options always; reveal advanced options on demand via expansion rather than hiding by default in menus users must discover.
rule.rationale: Progressive disclosure reduces visual noise while keeping options discoverable through visible "More options" or "Advanced" controls — not hidden menus users must know to look for.
